_note: All numbers are from real Kaggle run completed 2026-06-16. Use these verbatim in the report.

model.name: Longformer-base-4096
model.architecture: Transformer with global attention, reads up to 4096 tokens — about 3,000 words
model.trained_on: 2,534 news articles labeled for constructiveness by human annotators
model.output: A score from 1 to 5. Scores of 4 or 5 are flagged as constructive candidates.

performance._recall_note: At calibrated threshold 0.5, the model catches 62 out of 99 constructive articles
performance._precision_note: When the model flags something as constructive, it is right 38% of the time — 2x the 17.5% base rate
performance.where_it_struggles: c_score=4 articles (borderline constructive) — the model misses most of these due to limited training data
performance.where_it_excels: c_score=1 and 2 (clearly conventional) — error rate under 2%
performance._spearman_note: The continuous score correlates moderately with human ratings — useful as a ranking tool even where binary prediction fails

xai_faithfulness._aopc_note: AOPC measures how much model confidence drops when the model's own top-ranked words are hidden. LIG highlights are 5.7x more informative than random word selection.
xai_faithfulness._stability_note: When the same article is paraphrased slightly, the word importance rankings stay moderately consistent (Spearman 0.60 across 20 articles)

length_bias.interpretation: Longer articles score slightly higher. The bias is statistically significant but modest — not strong enough to explain most predictions.

source_gap.interpretation: Precision differs by up to 12% between the two training source corpora. Below the 25% concern threshold.

sentiment_bias.interpretation: Constructive articles are slightly more positive in tone, but the effect is small (Cohen's d = 0.32, below 0.5 threshold).

name_sensitivity.interpretation: In 2 out of 3 articles, masking named entities (people, organizations) shifts the model score by more than 5 percentage points. The model is sensitive to WHO an article is about, not just HOW it is written.

sentence_showcase._note: Three sentence types showing what the model reads as constructive. attr is within-article normalized (0–1, where 1.0 = strongest signal in that article). prob is the article-level model confidence. Bars are not cross-article comparable.

type_1.label: Named initiative with forward-looking outcome
type_1.sentence: On the icy shores of the North Sea, a 'graveyard' under construction is raising the hopes of climate experts: soon, the site will house a small portion of the CO₂ emitted by European industry, thus preventing it from ending up in the atmosphere.
type_1.attr_note: Highest-attributed sentence in article 1942, and the strongest single-sentence constructive signal across all 12 case studies.
type_1.context: Opening sentence of a 2022 feature on Norway's Northern Lights carbon capture project. Named place, named actors, concrete future outcome, expert validation. Classic solutions journalism structure — the kind of sentence the model was trained to reward.

type_2.label: Citizens filling an institutional gap
type_2.sentence: Gas shortage and energy shortage? The Swiss government has done little to remedy the problem. Therefore, citizens are taking their own measures.
type_2.attr_note: Opening paragraph of article 2140. The article's single highest-attributed sentence is deeper in the text (a line about hourly power-grid shutdowns as crisis-plan escalation).
type_2.context: Opening of a Swiss article on the 2022 energy crisis. People are buying generators and stockpiling fuel while the government falls short. 'Citizens are taking their own measures' is the key constructive signal — ordinary people filling a gap left by institutions.

type_3.label: Collective action at historical scale
type_3.sentence: The cooperative movement was once at the forefront of the global struggle for consumer rights, affordable rents, and even... world peace.
type_3.attr_note: Opening sentence of article 2469. The article's highest-attributed sentence (attr=1.0) is deeper in the text — the 1912 anti-war Basel Declaration.
type_3.context: Opening of a long-form Swiss article tracing the cooperative movement from its 1912 anti-war origins to the present. The model responds to language of organized solidarity and universal stakes: 'global struggle', 'consumer rights', 'world peace'.

1942.story: Carbon capture graveyard on the North Sea shore
1942.key_sentence: On the icy shores of the North Sea, a 'graveyard' under construction is raising the hopes of climate experts: soon, the site will house a small portion of the CO2 emitted by European industry.
1942.why_it_works: Classic solutions journalism — a named technical initiative, a concrete outcome, a human-scale story. The model picks up on 'graveyard' (reframing), 'hopes', 'raising', 'unveiled' as constructiveness signals.

2421.story: Lithuanian TV talk show pairing living performers with deceased legends via AI
2421.key_sentence: Technology will help to make impossible duets come true in the musical talk show 'Dream Duets', which starts on Saturday on LRT TELEVISION.
2421.why_it_works: Entertainment news — no problem framed, no solution proposed, no community impact. The model correctly sees this as a conventional celebrity/entertainment story.

275.story: Social Democrats propose pan-European tax Europol to fight VAT fraud
275.why_it_fails: The article describes a political proposal — it has solution-like language ('coordination', 'strengthen controls', 'cross-border') but is primarily a political story, not solutions journalism. The model is misled by the policy-proposal framing.

1246.story: EU accused of complicity in violent pushback of migrants at Balkan border
1246.why_it_fails: A deeply reported, human-centered investigation — the kind of journalism the model should surface. But the language is heavy: 'accused', 'violent', 'illegal', 'die'. The model reads tone rather than depth.
